channel.title: Personal Finance on maxcini
channel.description: Recent content in Personal Finance on maxcini

item.title: Why I still drive a 2005 Ford Escape
item.description:
I’ve been driving a 2005 Ford Escape since highschool because it only costs me $167/mo… for everything.

I get questioned alot why I still drive my car; my family think its unsafe, my friends think its funny, and my coworkers think its lame (I work for an automotive company that’s not Ford). The answer is because its cheap!

Now of course, driving around an old, paid off vehicle with moderately good fuel efficiency will always be more cost effective mode of transportation than purchasing a new vehicle. opportunity cost, riding around by bike

Total Cost of Vehicle Ownership Calculation

  • include broken down calculation here
  • make an excel spreadsheet detialing total cost of ownership for others to caluclate their car’s TCO
  • cost per measured unit is usually best for life cycle cost comparison, in this case $/mi (what you pay for what you get)

Bad News Bears

The Beast has had its fair share of problems:

  • Exhaust leak - pipe broke in half due to rust (car is parked in driveway year-round). The fix = some sheet metal and 2 hose clamps. Although, the car is still louder than normal, hence its nickname, “The Beast.”
  • Broke rear shock mount - degraded wheel well seal lead to excessive rust from road spray, which seperated the shock mount from the body of the vehicle. The fix = replacemnet shock mount bracket + custom aluminum splint to repair vehicle body and provide adequete mounting surface (thanks Uncle Tom!).
  • Electrical issues - I think there is an electrical leak somewhere. The fix = disconnect the battery manually for prolonged periods (>few days) of not driving the vehicle.

The Good Life

I can live with the ugly because the benefits make it worth it:

  • Cheap insurance; my insurance (somehow) classifies the car as a station wagon, and because the car is low value, it’s not worth full coverage so I have the cheapest plan (PLPD).
  • I dont care if anyone dings or scratches the car. It already has a bunch of scratches, but that doesn’t even compare to the rust on the underbody.
  • No concerns about theft. In fact (I probably shouldn’t tell you this) but I almost never lock my car - who would want to steal my old POS in a lot full of newer, more valuable cars?

When to Upgrade Your Car?

In my opinion, it’s time to upgrade your car when it no longer meets your requirements. I was hesitant to say desired characteristics, because consumer preferences are not always “requirements.” However, I think we will always desire the next best vehicle in comparison to what we currently drive, despite if we are currently satisfied.

I think you already have an idea of what my ideal vehicle criteria is:

  • Does it get me from point A to point B?
  • Is it safe?
  • Is it reliable (enough)?

For me, I plan on driving my ‘05 Escape into the ground. It has it’s problems, but so far (knock on wood) The Beast has held up. There’s no incentive for me to upgrade at this time considering my Total Cost of Ownership will increase with the purchase of a new vehicle.

That being said, I wouldn’t be opposed of a well kept, good used car. After all, majority of millionaires drive used cars according to the Millionaire Next Door by Thomas Stanley and William Danko. The only major benefit I think could justify such purchase is that the ‘05 Escape is 2 decades old, and safety features have rapidly advanced. I never concern myself with things out of my control, but, if the worst were to happen, I suppose it would be smart to be in a safer car.

Other

  • tips on leasing vs buying used vs buying new
  • in regards to gas vs electric cars, personally, I dont have a powertrain preference. I only care about getting from point A to point B that is safe, cheap, and reliable.
